task: Module 12 watchdog.ps1 add-content — file-landing SLA detection
what_worked: Gatekeeper and Thinker caught the corruption quickly during 'test it' request
Multiple agents diagnosed and fixed in parallel
Admin confirmed final syntax OK + Scheduled Task last result = 0
Watchdog restored to operational within ~15 minutes of discovery
what_failed: Creator's Add-Content for Module 12 catch block embedded the ENTIRE script header as a string literal inside the catch block
Result: } catch { Write-Host "Module 12 error: # watchdog.ps1 - OpenClaw Agent Health..." (900+ lines embedded)
Watchdog was non-functional for ~2 hours (12:23-14:35 UTC) without detection
Multiple agents (Creator, Gatekeeper, Thinker) all 'fixed' it in parallel — duplicate effort + duplicate Slack posts
Creator posted internal reasoning narration to Slack thread (streaming_state visible)
lesson: NEVER use Add-Content or write the entire script content into a catch block error message. For catch blocks, the error string must be a simple literal like '} catch { Write-Host "Module 12 error: $_" }'. Always read the EXACT target block immediately before editing large PowerShell files. Run syntax check immediately after any watchdog edit.
root_cause: Add-Content with Here-String or variable interpolation caused the entire script content to be appended inside the catch error string. The edit tool's exact-match mechanism grabbed too much context.
prevention: Run: Get-Content watchdog.ps1 | Select-Object -Last 20 BEFORE every edit to see current state

After every watchdog.ps1 change: run pwsh -Command 'Get-Content watchdog.ps1 | Out-Null; $null = [System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser]::ParseFile("C:\Users\azureuser\shared\scripts\watchdog.ps1", [ref]$null, [ref]$null)'
Use $_ in catch blocks, never interpolate multi-line strings
If watchdog.ps1 > 1100 lines, something is wrong — file size check is a fast sanity gate
user_feedback: Xiaosong: 'fix it' (directed at team) + 'remember whole discussion and process into memory and files'
